steps.title: Identify the boundary between two independent clauses
steps.content: The sentence contains two main independent clauses separated by a transition phrase. First clause: “the 2021 exhibition…set out to provide a wide-ranging overview of photography by women in the 1920s through the ______” Second clause: “its efforts were largely successful” Between them sits “given the collection's breadth of more than 120 photos,” which introduces the reason why the exhibition's efforts succeeded. This is the boundary where we need to connect the two independent clauses.

steps.title: Apply the rule for connecting independent clauses
steps.content: Two independent clauses must be joined by a comma followed by a coordinating conjunction (or a semicolon, or a period). The phrase “given the collection's breadth of more than 120 photos” functions as an introductory adverbial phrase that explains why the second clause is true—it belongs to the second clause, not between them. Therefore, we need “1950s, and” to properly signal that we are connecting two related but distinct independent ideas: what the exhibition aimed to do, and what actually resulted from it. Choice B also clarifies that these are two separate statements joined by a coordinator, not a single run-on thought. Choice C (comma alone) creates ambiguity about whether the clauses are properly separated; choices A and D lack the necessary comma-conjunction pairing to link independent clauses correctly. That matches choice B — B is correct.
